Enumerations follow the Jāmiʿ at-Tirmidhī (no. 3507) and Sunan Ibn Mājah (no. 3861) transmissions. Per SPEC.md §9, a qualified scholar should confirm the text before public release.

names.meaning_long:
Allāh is not one name among the ninety-nine so much as the ground on which all the others stand. It is treated as God's proper name — the one that gathers every attribute of perfection into a single reference, rather than pointing to any single quality the way "the Merciful" or "the Just" does. Where the other names each name a facet of divinity, this name names the One who possesses every facet at once, without partner or peer.

To hold this name in mind is to be recalled, again and again, from any partial idea of God back to the whole: every mercy, every power, every act of care in the world is, in the end, an act of *this* One, not of many. The proper response is neither analysis nor comparison but simple, wondering acknowledgment — that there is a reality answering to this name, and that everything else in existence depends on it.

quran_refs.text_ar: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ
quran_refs.text_en: In the name of Allāh,[2] the Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful.[3]

names.meaning_long:
Ar-Raḥmān points to a mercy so wide that it reaches every creature before there is any asking — sun and rain, breath and growth, the ordinary furniture of existence that sustains believer and unbeliever, the grateful and the heedless, alike. It is mercy as an all-encompassing provision built into the very structure of the world, not a mercy that waits to be earned.

A human being can never possess this quality in its fullness, since none of us gives existence itself. But there is a modest echo available: to attend to the needs of others before they voice them, and to let one's kindness extend past those who have earned it, toward the stranger and even the ungrateful, in imitation — however small — of a mercy that does not first calculate desert.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ
quran_refs.text_en: The Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful,

quran_refs.text_ar: قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ أَوِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ ۖ أَيًّا مَّا تَدْعُوا۟ فَلَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَلَا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلَاتِكَ وَلَا تُخَافِتْ بِهَا وَٱبْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا
quran_refs.text_en: Say, "Call upon Allāh or call upon the Most Merciful [ar-Raḥmān]. Whichever [name] you call - to Him belong the best names." And do not recite [too] loudly in your prayer or [too] quietly but seek between that an [intermediate] way.

names.meaning_long:
Where Ar-Raḥmān names mercy in its broadest, most general sweep, Ar-Raḥīm points to a more particular mercy — one bestowed specifically, and often kept for those who turn toward God in faith. It is the mercy of final outcomes: forgiveness given, distress relieved, a good end secured for those who seek it, rather than mercy simply diffused across all existing things.

The human share in this name is the deliberate, particular kindness a person directs toward someone in need — not the passive backdrop of general goodwill, but a mercy chosen and acted on for this person, in this moment, because their situation calls for it.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ
quran_refs.text_en: The Entirely Merciful, the Especially Merciful,

names.meaning_long:
Al-Malik names a sovereignty that needs nothing from what it rules. A king in the ordinary sense still depends on subjects, treasuries, allies; true kingship, in this name's sense, belongs only to the One who is wholly independent of all things while all things remain wholly dependent on Him — the reverse relation holding in every direction at once.

The corresponding discipline for a person is to loosen the grip of dependence on what one owns or controls — to want less from creation, not because possessions are evil, but because peace of heart grows in proportion to how little one actually needs from anyone besides God.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign,[1658] the Pure,[1659] the Perfection,[1660] the Grantor of Security,[1661] the Overseer,[1662] the Exalted in Might,[1663] the Compeller,[1664] the Superior.[1665] Exalted is Allāh above whatever they associate with Him.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Quddūs names a purity that outstrips not only the defects we can see, but every defect the mind can even imagine attaching to greatness — limitation, composition, resemblance to created things, change over time. It is holiness in the sense of being categorically set apart from anything a human concept of perfection could fully capture.

For a person, the resonance of this name is an invitation to inward purification: clearing the heart of envy, malice, and attachment to what is base, not because holiness can be manufactured, but because a heart cluttered with lesser things has no room left to recognize the Holy.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign,[1658] the Pure,[1659] the Perfection,[1660] the Grantor of Security,[1661] the Overseer,[1662] the Exalted in Might,[1663] the Compeller,[1664] the Superior.[1665] Exalted is Allāh above whatever they associate with Him.

names.meaning_long:
As-Salām carries two related senses: an essence entirely free of flaw, harm, or decay, and a giver of peace and safety to creation — the One from whom soundness flows and to whom the saved return in the abode named for this very quality.

A human share of this name shows up less in grand gestures than in small refusals: refusing to let one's hand or tongue become a source of harm to others, and cultivating a heart clear enough of grudges that those nearby can feel safe in one's presence.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign,[1658] the Pure,[1659] the Perfection,[1660] the Grantor of Security,[1661] the Overseer,[1662] the Exalted in Might,[1663] the Compeller,[1664] the Superior.[1665] Exalted is Allāh above whatever they associate with Him.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mu'min is the One who grants security — confirming truth in the hearts of those who believe, guaranteeing protection to those who trust in Him, and promising safety from ultimate injustice on the day all accounts are settled. It is security given, not merely felt: a guarantee backed by power to make good on it.

The corresponding human trait is trustworthiness — being someone whose word, once given, can be relied upon without hedge, and someone in whose presence others feel their rights and secrets are safe rather than at risk.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign,[1658] the Pure,[1659] the Perfection,[1660] the Grantor of Security,[1661] the Overseer,[1662] the Exalted in Might,[1663] the Compeller,[1664] the Superior.[1665] Exalted is Allāh above whatever they associate with Him.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muhaymin names a watchfulness that misses nothing — an oversight of every creature's state, need, and deed, sustaining and safeguarding all of it at once, the way a witness who never looks away also never fails to act on what is seen.

No human vigilance can approach this scope, but there is a narrower, honest analogue: the discipline of watching one's own state — one's intentions, words, and habits — with the same unbroken attention one would wish God's guardianship to model, rather than drifting through the day unexamined.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign,[1658] the Pure,[1659] the Perfection,[1660] the Grantor of Security,[1661] the Overseer,[1662] the Exalted in Might,[1663] the Compeller,[1664] the Superior.[1665] Exalted is Allāh above whatever they associate with Him.

names.meaning_long:
True 'izzah — the mightiness named here — requires three things together: being rare enough that not everyone attains it, being so needed that others cannot do without it, and being difficult enough to reach that it cannot simply be taken. Only God fulfills all three at once and without qualification; every worldly claim to might falls short on at least one count.

For a person, this name reframes where dignity is actually found — not in accumulating what others want, but in needing less from creation and answering only to God, which is a kind of mightiness available even to the materially poor.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign,[1658] the Pure,[1659] the Perfection,[1660] the Grantor of Security,[1661] the Overseer,[1662] the Exalted in Might,[1663] the Compeller,[1664] the Superior.[1665] Exalted is Allāh above whatever they associate with Him.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Jabbār carries two related meanings held together: the One whose will nothing can resist or overturn, and the One who mends what is broken — the same root that gives the word for setting a fractured bone. God compels the proud into submission and, in the same breath, restores what has been shattered in the lives of the afflicted.

A person's share is modest but real: mending the brokenhearted rather than adding to their fracture, and — turned inward — compelling one's own reluctant soul toward what is good when weakness or laziness resists it.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign,[1658] the Pure,[1659] the Perfection,[1660] the Grantor of Security,[1661] the Overseer,[1662] the Exalted in Might,[1663] the Compeller,[1664] the Superior.[1665] Exalted is Allāh above whatever they associate with Him.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mutakabbir names a greatness so total that everything besides God appears small by comparison — a claim to supremacy that would be arrogance and falsehood in any created being, since no creature actually possesses the greatness it might claim, but is simple truth in God, who alone possesses it in full.

There is no human share of this name to cultivate — pride in a person is a defect precisely because it claims a station not truly held. The honest response is the opposite motion: to recognize one's own smallness clearly enough that the ego's claims to greatness quiet down.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign,[1658] the Pure,[1659] the Perfection,[1660] the Grantor of Security,[1661] the Overseer,[1662] the Exalted in Might,[1663] the Compeller,[1664] the Superior.[1665] Exalted is Allāh above whatever they associate with Him.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Khāliq points to the One who brings things into being according to a due measure — first estimating and ordaining what a thing will be, in wisdom and precise proportion, before it is ever given existence. It is often read alongside Al-Bāri' and Al-Muṣawwir as three phases of one act: measured planning, distinct origination, and particular shaping.

Human making is never creation from nothing, and the distance here should be held honestly; still, the craftsman's or parent's care in shaping something well — attentive to proportion, order, and purpose — is a faint, creaturely echo of a measure the Creator alone truly holds.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, the Creator,[1666] the Producer,[1667] the Fashioner;[1668] to Him belong the best names.[1669] Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.[1670]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Bāri' names the One who brings each thing into existence free of any prior model to copy, and who gives every created thing its own distinct being, set apart from every other — the same root suggesting a kind of clearing or setting-free of one thing from another so that each has its own separate reality.

The takeaway for a person is less a practice to imitate than a matter of wonder: that no two things in creation, however similar, are without their own particular reality, each owing its distinctness to an Originator who needed no template at all.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, the Creator,[1666] the Producer,[1667] the Fashioner;[1668] to Him belong the best names.[1669] Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.[1670]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muṣawwir names the One who gives each created being its particular form — the shape, features, and arrangement that make it recognizably itself, and that make members of the same kind endlessly various rather than identical copies of one another.

A person's echo of this name lies in the care given to shaping one's own actions, work, and character well — attending to form and order, not only outcome — while remembering that the deepest shaping, of body and soul alike, belongs to God alone.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: He is Allāh, the Creator,[1666] the Producer,[1667] the Fashioner;[1668] to Him belong the best names.[1669] Whatever is in the heavens and earth is exalting Him. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.[1670]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ghaffār names a forgiveness that repeats without wearing thin — covering sin after sin, in this life and the next, as they recur, rather than a single pardon spent once and gone. The root sense of covering is important: the fault is hidden and its consequence withheld, not merely excused in passing.

The corresponding human quality is the willingness to overlook others' failings and to keep their secrets rather than expose them — a discipline of covering rather than broadcasting the faults one happens to know.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَإِنِّى لَغَفَّارٌ لِّمَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا ثُمَّ ٱهْتَدَىٰ
quran_refs.text_en: But indeed, I am the Perpetual Forgiver of whoever repents and believes and does righteousness and then continues in guidance.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Qahhār names an irresistible dominance — a power before which no tyrant's strength, no proud claim, and no rebellious will can ultimately stand. It is might that subdues not by contest but simply by being what it is; nothing in existence is positioned to resist it.

The nearest human parallel is turned inward rather than outward: subduing one's own unruly desires and the ego's demands, refusing to let appetite or pride dictate one's actions — a small, self-directed discipline rather than any dominance exercised over others.

quran_refs.text_ar: قُلْ مَن رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلْ أَفَٱتَّخَذْتُم مِّن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا ۚ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ أَمْ هَلْ تَسْتَوِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتُ وَٱلنُّورُ ۗ أَمْ جَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ خَلَقُوا۟ كَخَلْقِهِۦ فَتَشَـٰبَهَ ٱلْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّـٰرُ
quran_refs.text_en: Say, "Who is Lord of the heavens and earth?" Say, "Allāh." Say, "Have you then taken besides Him allies not possessing [even] for themselves any benefit or any harm?" Say, "Is the blind equivalent to the seeing? Or is darkness equivalent to light? Or have they attributed to Allāh partners who created like His creation so that the creation [of each] seemed similar to them?"[635] Say, "Allāh is the Creator of all things, and He is the One, the Prevailing."[636]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Wahhāb names giving in its purest form — a hiba, a gift given with no expectation of return and no underlying transaction, offered again and again out of sheer generosity rather than obligation or exchange. It is distinguished from giving that seeks benefit, praise, or reciprocity of any kind.

A person shares in this name by giving without calculating a return — even giving quietly, where no one will know to repay or praise it — since the moment a gift is given to secure something back, it has already left the territory this name describes.

quran_refs.text_ar: رَبَّنَا لَا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنَا وَهَبْ لَنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْوَهَّابُ
quran_refs.text_en: [Who say], "Our Lord, let not our hearts deviate after You have guided us and grant us from Yourself mercy. Indeed, You are the Bestower.[117]

names.meaning_long:
Ar-Razzāq names the One who both creates provision and delivers it to those who benefit from it — every form of sustenance, bodily and otherwise, including the nourishment of knowledge and guidance that feeds the heart the way food feeds the body.

The human share is to become a channel of provision for others rather than only a receiver of it — offering material help where one can, and offering useful knowledge or guidance where material means are limited, since both count as forms of rizq passed from one creature to another.

quran_refs.text_ar: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلرَّزَّاقُ ذُو ٱلْقُوَّةِ ٱلْمَتِينُ
quran_refs.text_en: Indeed, it is Allāh who is the [continual] Provider,[1548] the firm possessor of strength.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Fattāḥ names the One who opens what is closed — doors of provision, mercy, understanding, and guidance that no created effort alone can force open. The root also carries a sense of judging or deciding between truth and falsehood, opening a matter to its rightful resolution.

A person's echo of this name is practical: working to open what is stuck for others — resolving a disagreement, explaining what confuses someone, helping a stalled situation move — while recognizing that the deepest openings, of the heart especially, are ultimately God's to grant.

quran_refs.text_ar: قُلْ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا رَبُّنَا ثُمَّ يَفْتَحُ بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُوَ ٱلْفَتَّاحُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: Say, "Our Lord will bring us together; then He will judge between us in truth. And He is the Knowing Judge."[1232]

names.meaning_long:
Al-'Alīm names a knowledge that encompasses everything — hidden and manifest, past, present, and future — without ever being acquired, increased, or forgotten, unlike human knowledge, which is always gathered piecemeal, imperfect, and liable to lapse.

The corresponding human calling is the sincere pursuit of knowledge, especially knowledge of God, as among the noblest uses of the intellect — held always with the humility that whatever a person comes to know remains a small borrowing against a knowledge that is, in its true form, God's alone.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَسَوَّىٰهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ ۚ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: It is He who created for you all of that which is on the earth. Then He directed Himself[16] to the heaven, [His being above all creation], and made them seven heavens, and He is Knowing of all things.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Qābiḍ names the One who constricts — withholding provision from some as wisdom requires, and, in a related sense, drawing back the soul at the moment of death. It names a divine prerogative to tighten what might otherwise flow freely, always for reasons a limited human view cannot fully judge.

No person controls provision the way this name describes, so there is little to imitate directly; the honest response to one's own seasons of constriction — need, loss, hardship — is patient acceptance rather than resentment, trusting the same wisdom that also expands.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Bāsiṭ names the One who expands — opening up provision, widening hearts with faith and ease, granting more than strict calculation would predict. It is the counterpart to Al-Qābiḍ, showing that constriction and expansion both flow from the same wise hand.

A person can genuinely share something of this name by working to expand others rather than diminish them — widening someone's hope through encouragement, easing a tight situation through generosity, or simply making room in a hard moment rather than adding pressure to it.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Khāfiḍ names the One who brings low — humbling the arrogant, the unjust, and those who exalt themselves without right, regardless of how secure their position seemed. It is the sobering half of a pair completed by Ar-Rāfi', showing that rank in this world is entirely in God's disposal, not a fixed possession of the proud.

For a person, the lesson is cautionary rather than imitative: since abasement of the unjust belongs to God alone, the wiser posture is humility offered freely, rather than waiting to be humbled, and a corresponding care never to look down on others from a height one does not truly hold.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Ar-Rāfi' names the One who raises up — elevating the ranks of the faithful and the righteous, in knowledge, in reputation among the sincere, and ultimately in the degrees of the life to come, regardless of how the world happened to rank them.

The human parallel is to become someone who lifts others rather than one who competes to be lifted — supporting another's dignity, recognizing their worth, and helping them rise, especially when the world has not been generous to them.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mu'izz names the One who grants honor to whom He wills, on terms that owe nothing to worldly wealth, lineage, or status — honor rooted instead in faith, righteousness, and nearness to God, which can be given to the humblest person and withheld from the most powerful.

A person shares in this name by honoring others according to that same measure — valuing sincerity and goodness in someone regardless of their social standing, rather than reserving respect for those the world already deems important.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mudhill names the One who disgraces or humbles whom He wills — often those who reject truth, transgress against others, or exalt themselves wrongly — showing that no worldly power is secure against a lowering that only God can impose or remove.

This is a name to reflect on rather than imitate: recognizing that seeking honor from creation alone is unstable ground, and that the only sound response to another's disgrace is neither triumph nor scorn, but sober awareness that the same disposal of honor and shame governs everyone equally.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
As-Samī' names a hearing that takes in every sound — spoken and unspoken, loud and silent — without need of ears or any physical means, reaching even the quiet plea of a heart that never says a word aloud.

The human echo of this name is attentiveness: truly listening when someone speaks, especially the weak or distressed whose voices are easiest to miss, rather than only waiting for one's turn to respond.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَٰهِـۧمُ ٱلْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ ٱلْبَيْتِ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلُ رَبَّنَا تَقَبَّلْ مِنَّآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: And [mention] when Abraham was raising the foundations of the House and [with him] Ishmael, [saying], "Our Lord, accept [this] from us. Indeed, You are the Hearing,[42] the Knowing.[43]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Baṣīr names a seeing that takes in everything visible and hidden alike, without light or organ, reaching into the smallest and most concealed particulars of creation as fully as into what lies in plain view.

For a person, the corresponding discipline is a kind of moral insight — training oneself to notice what is easy to overlook: another's quiet suffering, an injustice done out of sight, the unseen consequences of one's own actions — a seeing that goes beyond the merely visible.

quran_refs.text_ar: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تُؤَدُّوا۟ ٱلْأَمَـٰنَـٰتِ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهَا وَإِذَا حَكَمْتُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ أَن تَحْكُمُوا۟ بِٱلْعَدْلِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ نِعِمَّا يَعِظُكُم بِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ سَمِيعًۢا بَصِيرًا
quran_refs.text_en: Indeed, Allāh commands you to render trusts to whom they are due and when you judge between people to judge with justice. Excellent is that which Allāh instructs you. Indeed, Allāh is ever Hearing and Seeing.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ḥakam names the One whose judgment is final and cannot be appealed or overturned — deciding rightly between truth and falsehood in every matter, unlike human judges, whose rulings, however careful, remain fallible and open to revision.

The human parallel is to judge fairly wherever one is called to arbitrate — setting bias and self-interest aside, and pursuing the truth of a matter as one's honest, if imperfect, aim.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-'Adl names a justice that does more than restore a balance already assumed to exist — it originates the very order in which everything is placed exactly where it belongs. Every decree and every act of God's justice is thus perfectly fitting, even where limited human understanding cannot yet see why.

A person's share of this name is the ordinary, demanding work of fairness — giving each person and each situation its rightful due, resisting favoritism, and refusing to let convenience or self-interest tilt a judgment one is entrusted to make.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Laṭīf names a gentleness and subtlety that reaches into the finest, most hidden particulars of creation — arranging good for creatures through means so delicate or concealed that the benefit often arrives without the recipient ever perceiving how it was brought about.

The human echo of this name is a considerate, unobtrusive kindness — attending to another's hidden needs before they are spoken, and offering help in ways gentle enough that it does not burden or embarrass the one receiving it.

quran_refs.text_ar: لَّا تُدْرِكُهُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ
quran_refs.text_en: Vision perceives Him not,[332] but He perceives [all] vision; and He is the Subtle,[333] the Aware.[334]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Khabīr names a knowledge that reaches the inmost reality of things — not only what appears on the surface, but the hidden how and why beneath it, an experiential depth of awareness that surface observation alone could never supply.

For a person, this name invites a habit of looking past first impressions — trying to understand the real motives and circumstances behind what others do, rather than settling for the easiest, most surface-level reading of a situation.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ
quran_refs.text_en: And He is the subjugator over His servants. And He is the Wise,[296] the Aware.[297]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ḥalīm names a forbearance that withholds punishment despite full awareness of wrongdoing and full power to punish at once — giving the wrongdoer time and opportunity to turn back, rather than a forbearance born of indifference or ignorance, as human patience so often is.

The corresponding discipline for a person is restraint in anger — holding back from retaliation one has the means to carry out, and giving others the same room to reconsider and repair that one would hope for oneself.

quran_refs.text_ar: لَّا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: Allāh does not impose blame upon you for what is unintentional in your oaths, but He imposes blame upon you for what your hearts have earned. And Allāh is Forgiving and Forbearing.[84]

names.meaning_long:
Al-'Aẓīm names a greatness of rank and reality that exceeds anything intellects can fully grasp — not greatness of size or extent, which belongs to bodies, but a magnitude of station that leaves human comprehension short no matter how far it reaches.

For a person, this name is less something to imitate than something to be humbled by — cultivating a sense of awe that keeps worldly grandeur, wealth, and power in proportion, remembering that none of it approaches true greatness, which belongs to God alone.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: Allāh - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living,[98] the Self-Sustaining.[99] Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them,[100] and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursī[101] extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High,[102] the Most Great.[103]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ghafūr shares its root (*gh-f-r*, "to cover, to veil") with several other names of forgiveness, and al-Ghazālī treats it as a close companion to Al-Ghaffār. Where the intensified form emphasizes the *repetition* of forgiveness — a covering renewed with every fresh fault — Al-Ghafūr points more simply to the reality of the covering itself: God veils the sin from view, in this world and the next, and lifts the fear of exposure and punishment that would otherwise attend it.

Al-Ghazālī is careful to note that this veiling is not indifference to wrong; it is a deliberate mercy that spares the servant humiliation while the door to correction stays open. He draws out the human share of the name in the practice of *sitr* — covering the faults of others rather than parading them, and forgiving without insisting on exposure or repayment in kind.

quran_refs.text_ar: إِنَّمَا حَرَّمَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةَ وَٱلدَّمَ وَلَحْمَ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ بِهِۦ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ بَاغٍ وَلَا عَادٍ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: He has only forbidden to you dead animals,[60] blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been dedicated to other than Allāh. But whoever is forced [by necessity], neither desiring [it] nor transgressing [its limit], there is no sin upon him. Indeed, Allāh is Forgiving and Merciful.

names.meaning_long:
Ash-Shakūr is God's gratitude toward His servants — a striking reversal, since gratitude ordinarily flows from the lesser to the greater. Al-Ghazālī explains it as God's readiness to notice even a small act of obedience and to reward it far beyond its worth, multiplying a fleeting effort into lasting recompense, and never letting sincere good go unacknowledged.

He draws a deliberate contrast with human gratitude, which is owed to God for favors that can never really be repaid, and which is itself only possible because God enables it. The human share of this name, in al-Ghazālī's reading, is to appreciate the good done by others — even in small measure — and to acknowledge it openly rather than taking kindness for granted.

quran_refs.text_ar: لِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدَهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ
quran_refs.text_en: That He may give them in full their rewards and increase for them of His bounty. Indeed, He is Forgiving and Appreciative.[1250]

names.meaning_long:
Al-'Alī, the Most High, is not height of place — al-Ghazālī is emphatic that God is not elevated the way a mountain or a throne is elevated. The loftiness meant here is a transcendence of rank and perfection: God's being and attributes stand utterly above every deficiency, comparison, and creaturely limitation that could otherwise be imagined alongside Him.

Al-Ghazālī links this to the broader Ghazālian theme that spatial language applied to God must always be reread as language about incomparability. The human echo of the name lies in rising above what is base — bodily appetite, petty rivalry — toward a loftiness of character built on knowledge and sincere worship, an ascent of the soul rather than of the body.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: Allāh - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living,[98] the Self-Sustaining.[99] Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them,[100] and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursī[101] extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High,[102] the Most Great.[103]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Kabīr, the Most Great, is greatness as the intellect perceives it rather than as the eye measures it. Al-Ghazālī distinguishes the greatness proper to bodies — which is really just bulk or extent — from the greatness proper to God, which is a perfection of attribute and rank recognized only by reflection, never by the senses.

He treats this name alongside others of majesty (Al-Jalīl, Al-Mutakabbir) as part of a family describing a grandeur that dwarfs any creaturely comparison. For the servant, the name teaches a reordering of what one calls "great": true greatness of a person lies in knowledge, justice, and generosity of soul, not in stature, wealth, or worldly power — and even these human forms of greatness are only faint echoes of God's.

quran_refs.text_ar: عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ٱلْكَبِيرُ ٱلْمُتَعَالِ
quran_refs.text_en: [He is] Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Grand, the Exalted.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ḥafīẓ, the Preserver, carries a double sense in al-Ghazālī's account: God preserves creation from dissolution moment to moment — holding the heavens from falling, sustaining bodies in being — and God preserves the record of every deed, so that nothing done in secret is lost before the reckoning.

Both senses meet in the idea of a watchfulness that never lapses and a memory that never fails. Al-Ghazālī draws from this the human duty of safeguarding what is entrusted to us — promises, secrets, the wellbeing of those in our care — as a small, faithful reflection of the preservation that sustains the whole of creation.

quran_refs.text_ar: فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقَدْ أَبْلَغْتُكُم مَّآ أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِۦٓ إِلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَيَسْتَخْلِفُ رَبِّى قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ وَلَا تَضُرُّونَهُۥ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ
quran_refs.text_en: But if you turn away, then I have already conveyed that with which I was sent to you. My Lord will give succession to a people other than you, and you will not harm Him at all. Indeed my Lord is, over all things, Guardian."[560]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muqīt, the Nourisher, is bound to the Arabic *qūt* — the sustenance that keeps a body alive — and al-Ghazālī reads it as God's precise apportioning of nourishment to every creature according to its need, down to the smallest living thing whose provision might otherwise seem overlooked.

He also notes a second shade carried by the Qur'anic verse in which the name occurs (4:85), where it can suggest a watchful sufficiency — God as the one "having power over" or attentively present to all things, not merely as feeder but as the one whose care makes provision arrive exactly where and when it is needed. The human share is a felt responsibility for those who depend on us for their sustenance, given without waste or neglect.

quran_refs.text_ar: مَّن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ نَصِيبٌ مِّنْهَا ۖ وَمَن يَشْفَعْ شَفَـٰعَةً سَيِّئَةً يَكُن لَّهُۥ كِفْلٌ مِّنْهَا ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقِيتًا
quran_refs.text_en: Whoever intercedes for a good cause will have a share [i.e., reward] therefrom; and whoever intercedes for an evil cause will have a portion [i.e., burden] therefrom. And ever is Allāh, over all things, a Keeper.[205]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ḥasīb carries two meanings that al-Ghazālī draws together: the One who *reckons* — computing every deed, however minute, with perfect accuracy — and the One who *suffices* — from the same root sense in which something "is enough" for a person, so that trust in Him removes the need to seek security elsewhere.

Read this way, the name binds precision of judgment to sufficiency of care: the same God who counts every act exactly is also the God in whom reliance is never misplaced. Al-Ghazālī draws the human share from both halves — scrupulous honesty in reckoning one's own conduct, and a settled contentment (tawakkul) that does not run anxiously after security God has already promised to provide.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَٱبْتَلُوا۟ ٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغُوا۟ ٱلنِّكَاحَ فَإِنْ ءَانَسْتُم مِّنْهُمْ رُشْدًا فَٱدْفَعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا تَأْكُلُوهَآ إِسْرَافًا وَبِدَارًا أَن يَكْبَرُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن كَانَ غَنِيًّا فَلْيَسْتَعْفِفْ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ فَقِيرًا فَلْيَأْكُلْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۚ فَإِذَا دَفَعْتُمْ إِلَيْهِمْ أَمْوَٰلَهُمْ فَأَشْهِدُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ حَسِيبًا
quran_refs.text_en: And test the orphans [in their abilities] until they reach marriageable age. Then if you perceive in them sound judgement, release their property to them. And do not consume it excessively and quickly, [anticipating] that they will grow up. And whoever, [when acting as guardian], is self-sufficient should refrain [from taking a fee]; and whoever is poor - let him take according to what is acceptable. Then when you release their property to them, bring witnesses upon them. And sufficient is Allāh as Accountant.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Jalīl, the Majestic, names a greatness that inspires reverence rather than mere recognition — a grandeur of essence and attribute so complete that it evokes awe in whoever perceives it, even faintly. Al-Ghazālī treats it as closely allied to Al-Kabīr, the two together describing a majesty that combines magnitude with a kind of sanctity that keeps it beyond familiarity.

This name is attested in the Sunnah rather than the Qur'an directly, and al-Ghazālī's comments on it are correspondingly brief, mostly folded into his broader discussion of greatness-names. The human share he draws is a disposition toward the dignified and the noble in one's own conduct, and a corresponding distaste for pettiness — a small imitation of jalāl in character.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ghazālī gives Al-Karīm, the Generous, some of his most developed attention among these names. True generosity (*karam*), he argues, is giving that asks nothing back and needs no motive outside itself — not generosity purchased by flattery, not generosity performed for praise, but a giving that flows freely because giving is simply what befits the Giver. He distinguishes lesser generosities, which are always entangled with some benefit to the giver, from God's, which precedes any request and continues even toward those who never ask.

He extends this to include the overlooking of faults: a generous one does not merely give, but forgives and spares without needing to exact what is owed. The human share is to give before being asked, to help without expecting thanks, and to let one's generosity be shaped less by calculation and more by simple largeness of heart.

quran_refs.text_ar: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَا غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ ٱلْكَرِيمِ
quran_refs.text_en: O mankind, what has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous,

names.meaning_long:
Ar-Raqīb, the Watchful, joins two things al-Ghazālī treats as inseparable in God: total knowledge and unbroken attentiveness. To watch over something is not simply to know about it once, but to remain present to it continuously — nothing drifts out of view, nothing is momentarily forgotten while attention turns elsewhere.

From this, al-Ghazālī draws the practice of *murāqaba* — the servant's cultivated awareness of always being seen, which shapes conduct in private exactly as it would in public. This is close to the well-known description of worshiping God "as though you see Him," and it gives Ar-Raqīb a practical weight beyond the merely descriptive: it is a name meant to be felt in the choices no one else observes.

quran_refs.text_ar: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًا كَثِيرًا وَنِسَآءً ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى تَسَآءَلُونَ بِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْحَامَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا
quran_refs.text_en: O mankind, fear your Lord, who created you from one soul and created from it its mate and dispersed from both of them many men and women. And fear Allāh, through whom[156] you ask one another,[157] and the wombs.[158] Indeed Allāh is ever,[159] over you, an Observer.[160]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mujīb, the Responsive, is God's readiness to answer the one who calls — whether the caller is aware of asking or not, whether the answer takes the shape requested or something better suited to the caller's real good. Al-Ghazālī frames this responsiveness as meeting sincerity rather than mere technique; it is not that certain words compel an answer, but that a turning heart finds itself met.

He draws from this a clear human obligation: to be responsive in turn to those who call upon us for help, answering readily rather than delaying or requiring elaborate persuasion. A person who reflects Al-Mujīb is quick to meet a genuine need, as God is quick to meet the sincere supplicant.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ هُوَ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيهَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى قَرِيبٌ مُّجِيبٌ
quran_refs.text_en: And to Thamūd [We sent] their brother Ṣāliḥ. He said, "O my people, worship Allāh; you have no deity other than Him. He has produced you from the earth and settled you in it, so ask forgiveness of Him and then repent to Him. Indeed, my Lord is near and responsive."

names.meaning_long:
Al-Wāsi', the All-Encompassing, describes a vastness that is not spatial but a boundlessness of knowledge, mercy, and giving. Al-Ghazālī reads it as the assurance that nothing exhausts God's capacity to know, to forgive, or to provide — no multitude of needs crowds out any single one, and no immensity of sin outstrips the reach of His mercy.

Because the vastness described is one of generosity rather than mere extent, al-Ghazālī draws from it a caution against narrowness in the servant: a call to widen one's own knowledge, tolerance, and giving rather than hoarding them defensively, in however small a measure a creature can mirror what is, in God, without any measure at all.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَشْرِقُ وَٱلْمَغْرِبُ ۚ فَأَيْنَمَا تُوَلُّوا۟ فَثَمَّ وَجْهُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: And to Allāh belongs the east and the west. So wherever you [might] turn, there is the Face[37] of Allāh. Indeed, Allāh is all-Encompassing and Knowing.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ḥakīm, the Perfectly Wise, receives sustained attention from al-Ghazālī, who ties *ḥikma* (wisdom) to knowing the noblest realities by the noblest means and ordering all things toward their most fitting end. Wisdom, in his account, is not cleverness or mere competence; it is knowledge joined perfectly to right action, so that what is known is also enacted in the best possible way.

He treats God's wisdom as evident throughout creation — the fitting arrangement of causes and effects, the precise placement of every part relative to the whole — and distinguishes it from the more limited wisdom available to human beings, which is real but partial. The human share of the name lies in seeking sound knowledge and acting on it with care and proportion, letting insight actually govern conduct rather than remaining idle.

quran_refs.text_ar: قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: They said, "Exalted are You; we have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed, it is You who is the Knowing,[19] the Wise."[20]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Wadūd, the Loving, is distinguished by al-Ghazālī from Ar-Raḥmān: mercy responds to need, while love wills good for its own sake and draws the beloved near. He describes God's love as directed toward the obedient and the sincere, willing their good and elevating them in nearness — and describes God, too, as *beloved*, since the servant who glimpses something of the divine beauty and generosity cannot help but be drawn toward it.

This double sense — loving and being loved — gives Al-Wadūd a particular warmth among the names. Al-Ghazālī's human application is straightforward: to love others for the sake of their good, seeking their benefit rather than one's own advantage, and to let sincerity rather than self-interest be the root of one's affections.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ
quran_refs.text_en: And ask forgiveness of your Lord and then repent to Him. Indeed, my Lord is Merciful and Affectionate."

quran_refs.text_ar: وَهُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلْوَدُودُ
quran_refs.text_en: And He is the Forgiving, the Affectionate,

names.meaning_long:
Al-Majīd, the Most Glorious, joins together two things al-Ghazālī elsewhere treats separately: greatness of essence and abundance of generosity. Where Al-Kabīr and Al-Jalīl emphasize magnitude, and Al-Karīm emphasizes giving, Al-Majīd names their union — a glory that is not bare grandeur but grandeur expressed through unceasing generosity.

He treats the name as a kind of summation, gathering strands from the majesty-names and the giving-names into a single description of nobility perfected in both dimensions at once. The human share is a character that unites the two: not greatness without generosity, which curdles into pride, and not generosity without dignity, but a nobility that holds both together.

quran_refs.text_ar: قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَعْجَبِينَ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ رَحْمَتُ ٱللَّهِ وَبَرَكَـٰتُهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ حَمِيدٌ مَّجِيدٌ
quran_refs.text_en: They said, "Are you amazed at the decree of Allāh? May the mercy of Allāh and His blessings be upon you, people of the house. Indeed, He is Praiseworthy and Honorable."

quran_refs.text_ar: ذُو ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْمَجِيدُ
quran_refs.text_en: Honorable Owner of the Throne,

names.meaning_long:
Al-Bā'ith, the Resurrector, is treated by al-Ghazālī primarily in its most literal sense: the One who raises the dead on the Last Day, restoring bodies and souls for the reckoning that gives final meaning to every deed. He also allows a secondary, figurative sense — God "raising up" prophets and messengers to rouse heedless hearts, awakening people from the sleep of forgetfulness before the final Rising ever comes.

Attested through the Sunnah's list of names rather than a direct Qur'anic occurrence in this exact form, al-Ghazālī's comments here are relatively brief. The human share he suggests is modest but real: to be a means of rousing others — through honest reminder and good counsel — from heedlessness, in however small a way, before the greater Rising.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Ash-Shahīd, the Witness, describes a presence to all things that needs no mediation — no report, no inference, no gap between event and knowledge. Al-Ghazālī treats this witnessing as continuous with God's total knowledge, but with the added sense of direct, immediate presence: nothing hidden or manifest occurs without God being, in the fullest sense, witness to it as it happens.

He draws from this a clear practical bearing: the servant who truly believes in Ash-Shahīd cannot easily bear false witness or speak carelessly, since human testimony is always given, ultimately, before a Witness who cannot be deceived. The human share is honesty in bearing witness, and a felt accountability that shapes speech even when no other observer is present.

quran_refs.text_ar: مَّآ أَصَابَكَ مِنْ حَسَنَةٍ فَمِنَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَمَآ أَصَابَكَ مِن سَيِّئَةٍ فَمِن نَّفْسِكَ ۚ وَأَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ لِلنَّاسِ رَسُولًا ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًا
quran_refs.text_en: What comes to you of good is from Allāh, but what comes to you of evil, [O man], is from yourself.[201] And We have sent you, [O Muḥammad], to the people as a messenger, and sufficient is Allāh as Witness.[202]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ḥaqq, the Truth, is treated by al-Ghazālī in a register close to metaphysics: God is the Truth in the sense of being the one whose existence is real, necessary, and self-standing, while everything else exists only in a borrowed, dependent way. Where created things could be said not to exist, or to pass away, God's existence admits no such possibility — this is what makes Him "true" in the deepest sense, as opposed to the transient and the vain (*bāṭil*).

He also allows the more familiar sense of truth as opposed to falsehood — God's speech, promise, and judgment are all true without qualification. The human share is a double fidelity: to speak truthfully, and to orient one's life toward what is real and lasting rather than what merely glitters and passes.

quran_refs.text_ar: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّهُۥ يُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَأَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
quran_refs.text_en: That is because Allāh is the True Reality and because He gives life to the dead and because He is over all things competent

names.meaning_long:
Al-Wakīl, the Trustee, is the One who takes full and sufficient charge of the affairs of those who rely on Him. Al-Ghazālī develops this in close connection with *tawakkul* (reliance/trust): to know God as Al-Wakīl is to recognize that ultimate responsibility for one's outcomes can be placed in hands more capable and more caring than one's own, without this relieving a person of the ordinary effort required of them.

He is careful that trust of this kind is not passivity; it is confidence that one's sincere effort is held within a larger, trustworthy care. The human share of the name is twofold: to become someone others can rely on when entrusted with their affairs, and to place one's own deepest reliance in God rather than in the uncertain guarantees of the created world.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱلَّذِينَ قَالَ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱخْشَوْهُمْ فَزَادَهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ وَنِعْمَ ٱلْوَكِيلُ
quran_refs.text_en: Those to whom people [i.e., hypocrites] said, "Indeed, the people have gathered against you, so fear them." But it [merely] increased them in faith, and they said, "Sufficient for us is Allāh, and [He is] the best Disposer of affairs."[150]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Qawī, the All-Powerful, names a strength admitting no weakness, fatigue, or limit — power that is not merely sufficient for a task but entirely unconstrained by any obstacle whatsoever. Al-Ghazālī treats it alongside Al-Qādir (the Able) but with a sharper emphasis on might and intensity rather than mere capacity: Al-Qawī is strength considered in itself, not just strength as it relates to a particular act.

For the servant, this name reorients where strength is sought: not in one's own limited resources, which tire and fail, but in reliance on the source of all real power. Al-Ghazālī's implicit human lesson is a humility about self-sufficiency paired with confidence drawn from dependence on God.

quran_refs.text_ar: فَلَمَّا جَآءَ أَمْرُنَا نَجَّيْنَا صَـٰلِحًا وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَعَهُۥ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنَّا وَمِنْ خِزْىِ يَوْمِئِذٍ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ
quran_refs.text_en: So when Our command came, We saved Ṣāliḥ and those who believed with him, by mercy from Us, and [saved them] from the disgrace of that day.[561] Indeed, it is your Lord who is the Powerful, the Exalted in Might.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Matīn, the Firm, intensifies the sense of power found in Al-Qawī: this is strength exercised without any strain, resistance, or effort, an execution of will so complete that nothing pushes back against it. Al-Ghazālī treats the two names as forming a pair — power in its bare fact (Al-Qawī) and power in its perfectly unstrained exercise (Al-Matīn).

The human share he draws is steadiness of resolve: a person who reflects something of Al-Matīn is not easily shaken from right action by hardship or temptation, holding a firmness of purpose that, while never approaching the divine reality, still takes it as a distant model.

quran_refs.text_ar: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلرَّزَّاقُ ذُو ٱلْقُوَّةِ ٱلْمَتِينُ
quran_refs.text_en: Indeed, it is Allāh who is the [continual] Provider,[1548] the firm possessor of strength.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Walī, the Protecting Friend, combines authority with intimacy: this is not a distant governance but a guardianship exercised in nearness, taking charge of the affairs of the believers the way a devoted friend takes up the concerns of someone they love. Al-Ghazālī reads *wilāya* as care that is both authoritative — able to act decisively on the beloved's behalf — and affectionate, drawing the cared-for one close rather than holding them at a governor's distance.

The human share is to take up something of this same posture toward those in one's care: to support and befriend the vulnerable not merely from duty, but as an expression of real closeness, mirroring, however imperfectly, the friendship God extends to those who turn to Him.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ ٱلْغَيْثَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا قَنَطُوا۟ وَيَنشُرُ رَحْمَتَهُۥ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ
quran_refs.text_en: And it is He who sends down the rain after they had despaired and spreads His mercy. And He is the Protector, the Praiseworthy.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ḥamīd, the Praiseworthy, is worthy of praise independent of any praiser — God's praiseworthiness does not depend on being recognized, and every praiseworthy quality found scattered among creatures ultimately traces back to Him as its source. Al-Ghazālī treats this as the completion of a circle: God is praised by creation, whether consciously (through worship) or simply through the perfection each thing displays in its own being, and this praise returns to the very source of all that is praiseworthy.

The human share is double: constant praise (*ḥamd*) as an act of worship, and a life shaped toward praiseworthy character — not performed for others' approval, but because such a life, in its small way, echoes the perfection it is praising.

quran_refs.text_ar: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَنفِقُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ وَمِمَّآ أَخْرَجْنَا لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَلَا تَيَمَّمُوا۟ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنْهُ تُنفِقُونَ وَلَسْتُم بِـَٔاخِذِيهِ إِلَّآ أَن تُغْمِضُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ
quran_refs.text_en: O you who have believed, spend from the good things which you have earned and from that which We have produced for you from the earth. And do not aim toward the defective therefrom, spending [from that] while you would not take it [yourself] except with closed eyes. And know that Allāh is Free of need and Praiseworthy.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muḥṣī, the Counter, describes a knowledge so exhaustive that it numbers every single thing without exception or omission — nothing is too small, too fleeting, or too scattered to escape being accounted for. Al-Ghazālī treats this as an intensification of divine knowledge in the direction of precision: not simply that God knows all things, but that He knows them enumerated, down to their least particulars.

Attested through the Sunnah's naming tradition, al-Ghazālī's remarks here are comparatively brief. The human share he draws is the practice of *muḥāsaba* — keeping careful account of one's own deeds, taking nothing as too minor to matter, in imitation of a reckoning that misses nothing.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mubdi', the Initiator, is the One who brings creation into being for the very first time, with no prior model to imitate and nothing preexisting to work from. Al-Ghazālī treats this as origination in the strictest sense — not fashioning from available material, but calling into existence what had no existence at all.

He pairs the name naturally with Al-Mu'īd (the Restorer), origination and return forming a single arc from first creation to resurrection. The human share is a humbling one: whatever good a person seems to originate in their own life ultimately depends on a capacity for origination that belongs to God alone, and gratitude rather than self-credit is the fitting response.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mu'īd, the Restorer, completes the pair begun with Al-Mubdi': having originated creation once, God restores it again after its dissolution, bringing back what had ceased to be in a manner just as decisive as the first creation. Al-Ghazālī reads this against the Qur'anic pattern "as He originated you, so you will return," treating resurrection as continuous with, not different in kind from, the original act of creation.

The human share he suggests is a disposition of hope grounded in this pattern: what has been broken or lost is not necessarily beyond restoration, and a person who reflects something of this name works, in small and partial ways, to repair what has fallen apart rather than assuming decline is final.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muḥyī, the Giver of Life, spans several registers in al-Ghazālī's treatment: the literal giving of life to inert matter (a body formed in the womb, a barren earth revived by rain), and the figurative giving of life to what is spiritually dead — hearts hardened by heedlessness, revived through knowledge and faith. He treats these not as unrelated metaphors but as one reality appearing at different levels of creation.

The human share, in his account, is to become an instrument of this second kind of life-giving: reviving others through sincere guidance, encouragement, and kindness, aware that the capacity to truly quicken a heart belongs finally to God, even when a person is the occasion for it.

quran_refs.text_ar: فَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِ رَحْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ كَيْفَ يُحْىِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَآ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
quran_refs.text_en: So observe the effects of the mercy of Allāh - how He gives life to the earth after its lifelessness. Indeed, that [same one] will give life to the dead, and He is over all things competent.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mumīt, the Taker of Life, is the necessary counterpart to Al-Muḥyī: the same power that gives life ordains its end at an appointed term, for every living thing without exception. Al-Ghazālī treats death not as a failure of divine care but as part of the same ordered pattern that gave life in the first place — nothing lives outside this rhythm, and nothing dies outside of it either.

The human share he draws is the remembrance of death (*dhikr al-mawt*) as a spiritual discipline: reflecting honestly on mortality tends to loosen a person's grip on vanity and worldly attachment, and to sharpen readiness for what comes after.

quran_refs.text_ar: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَقَالُوا۟ لِإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ إِذَا ضَرَبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَوْ كَانُوا۟ غُزًّى لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ عِندَنَا مَا مَاتُوا۟ وَمَا قُتِلُوا۟ لِيَجْعَلَ ٱللَّهُ ذَٰلِكَ حَسْرَةً فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ
quran_refs.text_en: O you who have believed, do not be like those who disbelieved and said about their brothers when they traveled through the land or went out to fight, "If they had been with us, they would not have died or have been killed," so Allāh makes that [misconception] a regret within their hearts. And it is Allāh who gives life and causes death, and Allāh is Seeing of what you do.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ḥayy, the Ever-Living, names a life that is complete in itself — without beginning, without end, without dependence on nourishment, growth, or any of the processes that sustain created life. Al-Ghazālī treats this life as foundational among the attributes: knowledge, will, and power all presuppose life as their condition, so that Al-Ḥayy underlies the very possibility of the names built upon it.

Paired in the Qur'an with Al-Qayyūm (2:255), and named in a hadith among the greatest of God's names, Al-Ḥayy carries unusual weight. The human share al-Ghazālī suggests is a kind of spiritual aliveness — an alert, attentive heart rather than one dulled by heedlessness, however distant this remains from the uncaused life it echoes.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: Allāh - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living,[98] the Self-Sustaining.[99] Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them,[100] and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursī[101] extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High,[102] the Most Great.[103]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Qayyūm, the Self-Subsisting, names existence that needs nothing outside itself to continue, and upon which everything else depends for its own continuance. Al-Ghazālī treats this as the completion of Al-Ḥayy: not only does God live without dependence, but all other existence — every creature, every moment of the created order — subsists only because it is sustained, instant by instant, by this self-subsisting life.

The pairing of these two names in the Throne Verse and elsewhere is, in his reading, no accident: together they express the total independence of God from creation and the total dependence of creation on Him. The human share is a clear-eyed recognition of one's own contingency — a corrective to any illusion of self-sufficiency, replaced by conscious reliance on the One who alone truly stands on His own.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: Allāh - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living,[98] the Self-Sustaining.[99] Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them,[100] and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursī[101] extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High,[102] the Most Great.[103]

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ
quran_refs.text_en: Allāh - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Self-Sustaining.[114]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Wājid, the Perceiver, is one of the names attested through the Sunnah's enumeration rather than a direct Qur'anic occurrence in this form, and al-Ghazālī's remarks on it are notably brief compared to names like Al-Ḥayy or Al-Karīm. The root sense is of finding or perceiving without seeking: God is never in a state of searching for what He lacks, since nothing real is absent from His knowledge or power — whatever He wills, He simply has, without the effort or uncertainty that finding implies for creatures.

Given how compressed the source treatment is here, this should be read as a more tentative sketch than the fuller entries above. The human share, offered cautiously, might be a contentment that comes from not restlessly searching for what one already has reason to trust will be provided.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
This entry, Al-Mājid ("The Illustrious"), shares its Arabic root — *m-j-d*, glory or nobility — with Al-Majīd ("The Most Glorious"), treated earlier among these names as a synthesis of greatness and generosity. The two forms are grammatically distinct (an active-participle pattern here versus the intensive pattern there), and some enumerations of the ninety-nine names include both as separate names, but al-Ghazālī's discussion of the *majd* root does not clearly separate them into two independently developed treatments.

Given that overlap, this should be read as provisional: the same themes of grandeur joined to generosity likely apply, and the human share — nobility of character that unites greatness with liberality — mirrors what was said of Al-Majīd. A scholar should confirm whether al-Ghazālī (or the wider tradition he draws on) in fact distinguishes these two forms with any independent content, since this draft cannot responsibly assert a distinction it did not clearly find.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Wāḥid, the One, denotes an absence of multiplicity in God's essence — no parts, no division, nothing that could be added or subtracted to make God "more" or "less" Himself. Al-Ghazālī discusses a fine distinction between Al-Wāḥid and Al-Aḥad (Absolute Oneness): where Al-Aḥad denies even the conceptual possibility of division, Al-Wāḥid is oneness considered as excluding actual multiplicity or partnership, especially the sharing of divine attributes with any other.

This is a subtle point of his method, and worth a scholar's careful confirmation rather than casual repetition. The human share he draws is an inward *tawḥīd* of intention — gathering a divided heart, torn between competing loyalties, into a single, undivided orientation toward God.

quran_refs.text_ar: قُلْ مَن رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلْ أَفَٱتَّخَذْتُم مِّن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا ۚ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ أَمْ هَلْ تَسْتَوِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتُ وَٱلنُّورُ ۗ أَمْ جَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ خَلَقُوا۟ كَخَلْقِهِۦ فَتَشَـٰبَهَ ٱلْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّـٰرُ
quran_refs.text_en: Say, "Who is Lord of the heavens and earth?" Say, "Allāh." Say, "Have you then taken besides Him allies not possessing [even] for themselves any benefit or any harm?" Say, "Is the blind equivalent to the seeing? Or is darkness equivalent to light? Or have they attributed to Allāh partners who created like His creation so that the creation [of each] seemed similar to them?"[635] Say, "Allāh is the Creator of all things, and He is the One, the Prevailing."[636]

names.meaning_long:
Aṣ-Ṣamad, occurring in Sūrat al-Ikhlāṣ (112:2), is often rendered "the Eternal" in English, though the classical sense al-Ghazālī draws on is closer to "the Master upon whom all depend": the one every creature turns to in need, who is Himself complete and in need of nothing and no one. This completeness is what makes Him a fitting object of every hope and every request — a court of appeal that never itself has recourse to another.

Al-Ghazālī reads this alongside the surrounding verses of al-Ikhlāṣ as underscoring God's utter independence from creation, contrasted with creation's constant dependence on Him. The human share is to direct one's deepest needs to this one true source rather than scattering hope among creatures who are, themselves, just as needy and dependent.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ
quran_refs.text_en: Allāh, the Eternal Refuge.[2012]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Qādir names the reality whose capacity to act meets no resistance, no fatigue, and no need for instrument or intermediary. Where a person's power is borrowed, partial, and dependent on tools, health, and circumstance, this power is entire in itself: to will a thing and for it to be, or to withhold it, without labor and without another's help.

Held alongside knowledge and will — the two capacities power always presupposes — Al-Qādir points to a capability that could bring anything possible into being or let it remain unmanifest, with no gap between decision and effect. For the one who reflects on it, the name is less an abstract claim about divine attributes than an invitation to notice how thin human power actually is, and to spend the small measure of power one does have — over one's own limbs, tongue, and choices — in service rather than in illusions of self-sufficiency.

quran_refs.text_ar: قُلْ هُوَ ٱلْقَادِرُ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ أَوْ مِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُم بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَفْقَهُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: Say, "He is the [one] Able to send upon you affliction from above you or from beneath your feet or to confuse you [so you become] sects[314] and make you taste the violence of one another." Look how We diversify the signs that they might understand.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muqtadir carries the same root as Al-Qādir but in an intensified form, and classical commentary on the Names often reads such intensified forms as pointing to a degree of a quality so complete that nothing could conceivably overmatch or resist it. If Al-Qādir affirms power as such, Al-Muqtadir affirms that this power faces no rival, no obstacle, and no ceiling — the mightiest of creation stand before it as nothing.

This is not power multiplied for its own display, but power described from the vantage of everything that might seem, to human eyes, unconquerable: death, empires, the vastness of the heavens. All of it is equally within reach of a single will. The believer's proper response is not fear alone but a settled trust — that whatever seems overwhelming in one's own life is not overwhelming to the One this name describes.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا تَذْرُوهُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحُ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقْتَدِرًا
quran_refs.text_en: And present to them the example of the life of this world, [its being] like rain which We send down from the sky, and the vegetation of the earth mingles with it[791] and [then] it becomes dry remnants, scattered by the winds. And Allāh is ever, over all things, Perfect in Ability.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muqaddim and its companion Al-Mu'akhkhir describe a single reality that orders precedence and delay throughout creation: which causes act before their effects, which souls are drawn nearer before others, which nations rise before others fall, which servant is granted an opening in this life or the next before someone else. Nothing moves forward by accident; what is advanced is advanced by decree, not chance.

Taken to heart, the name steadies the restlessness of comparison — the sense that another person's advancement, in wealth, recognition, or spiritual state, has come at one's own expense. Al-Ghazālī's broader habit of pairing opposite-sounding names as two faces of one governing wisdom suggests that here too, precedence and deferral together describe a single, unerring ordering, not two competing forces.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Where Al-Muqaddim brings forward, Al-Mu'akhkhir sets back — delaying a person's provision, a nation's reckoning, or an answer to prayer, not from negligence but from a wisdom that knows when a thing is timely and when it is not. What looks, from within a single lifetime, like a door closed or a request refused may in the larger ordering be something rightly deferred.

The two names are inseparable in meaning: one who did not delay could not properly be said to expedite, and vice versa. Read together, they ask for patience with timing one cannot see the whole of, and they quietly correct the temptation to treat delay as denial.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Awwal affirms a beginning that has no beginning before it — not first in a sequence of things that came before, since nothing came before, but first in the sense of being wholly unpreceded, the ground rather than the start of a series. Everything originated has, by definition, a moment or cause prior to it; this name describes the one reality for which no such "before" exists at all.

Al-Ghazālī's treatment of this name is closely bound to its counterpart, Al-Ākhir, and to the hadith pairing it with Aẓ-Ẓāhir and Al-Bāṭin — First and Last, Manifest and Hidden, named together in one saying as a single statement about transcending both time and the categories by which creatures are known. The four names read as one teaching: nothing encloses this reality, neither in duration nor in perceptibility.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱلْأَوَّلُ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرُ وَٱلظَّـٰهِرُ وَٱلْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: He is the First[1623] and the Last,[1624] the Ascendant[1625] and the Intimate,[1626] and He is, of all things, Knowing.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ākhir affirms an end that has no end after it — not last in a countdown toward extinction, but last in the sense of remaining when everything else has ceased, without itself being subject to cessation. Every created thing, however durable, moves toward some horizon of ending; this name describes what stands entirely outside that horizon.

Taken with Al-Awwal, the pairing dissolves the ordinary picture of a timeline with God somewhere within it. There is no "within." The believer who sits with this name is left less with a concept to master than with a felt sense of how small the span of any single life is against a reality unbounded on both ends.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱلْأَوَّلُ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرُ وَٱلظَّـٰهِرُ وَٱلْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: He is the First[1623] and the Last,[1624] the Ascendant[1625] and the Intimate,[1626] and He is, of all things, Knowing.

names.meaning_long:
Aẓ-Ẓāhir names a reality made evident by everything that exists: every ordered movement of the heavens, every intricacy of a living body, every turn of cause and effect is, on this reading, a kind of testimony — so pervasive and so constant that nothing could be more manifest. In this sense nothing is hidden from view the way this name's meaning is hidden from view; it is written across the whole of what is.

Yet the name is paired, almost paradoxically, with Al-Bāṭin, the Hidden — because the essence behind all this evidence remains inaccessible to sense and imagination, however plainly its traces are strewn everywhere. Al-Ghazālī's characteristic move is to hold both truths together rather than resolve the tension: everywhere apparent through effect, nowhere graspable in itself.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱلْأَوَّلُ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرُ وَٱلظَّـٰهِرُ وَٱلْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: He is the First[1623] and the Last,[1624] the Ascendant[1625] and the Intimate,[1626] and He is, of all things, Knowing.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Bāṭin names what lies beyond the reach of sense, imagination, and even careful reasoning — not hidden as a secret waiting to be uncovered by better instruments, but hidden as something categorically beyond what created faculties were built to perceive. No eye will see it in this life; no thought will fully encompass it in the next.

And yet this very hiddenness is presented alongside utter manifestness (Aẓ-Ẓāhir): the two are not in tension so much as describing two aspects of one reality from two different vantage points — as effect and as essence. Held together, they ask for humility about what the intellect can and cannot close its hand around, without lapsing into the claim that nothing can be known at all.

quran_refs.text_ar: هُوَ ٱلْأَوَّلُ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرُ وَٱلظَّـٰهِرُ وَٱلْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: He is the First[1623] and the Last,[1624] the Ascendant[1625] and the Intimate,[1626] and He is, of all things, Knowing.

names.meaning_long:
