Al-Wālī, here distinguished from the earlier name Al-Walī ("The Protecting Friend," وَلِيّ), denotes the one who directly administers and governs the affairs of all creation — not a friend drawing near, but a ruler running the affairs of the cosmos without minister, deputy, or need of counsel. Every motion of the stars, every turn of a life, unfolds under a single, unmediated governance.

Classical treatment of the ninety-nine names often notes this near-homograph pair as an example of how subtle differences in vowel pattern carry real differences in meaning — the loving nearness of a protector (Al-Walī) and the administrative oversight of a ruler (Al-Wālī) are related but distinct facets of the same root. This entry concerns the latter: God as the one who governs, without any need for an intermediary between decree and effect.

quran_refs.text_ar: لَهُۥ مُعَقِّبَـٰتٌ مِّنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ يَحْفَظُونَهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُغَيِّرُ مَا بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا۟ مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ ۗ وَإِذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوٓءًا فَلَا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ ۚ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَالٍ
quran_refs.text_en: For him [i.e., each one] are successive [angels][631] before and behind him who protect him by the decree of Allāh.[632] Indeed, Allāh will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves. And when Allāh intends for a people ill,[633] there is no repelling it. And there is not for them besides Him any patron.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muta'ālī names an exaltation carried to its furthest point — not simply high, but altogether above every category by which creatures are compared, located, or measured. It is elevation above resemblance, above containment in space or direction, above every imperfection that a limited mind might, out of habit, try to attach to the divine.

Al-Ghazālī's broader project in expounding the Names repeatedly returns to this kind of transcendence (tanzīh): stripping away every association drawn unconsciously from created experience. Al-Muta'ālī intensifies an already-exalted register (compare the earlier name Al-'Alī) into something like a final word on the matter — there is no vantage from which this reality could be diminished, compared, or brought down to size.

quran_refs.text_ar: عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ٱلْكَبِيرُ ٱلْمُتَعَالِ
quran_refs.text_en: [He is] Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Grand, the Exalted.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Barr describes a kindness that overflows what is owed — goodness given not as payment for merit but as sheer generosity, reaching the deserving and undeserving alike, the strong and especially the weak. It shares territory with Ar-Raḥmān but leans toward the picture of a benefactor whose gifts arrive quietly, often unnoticed, in provision, ease, and protection from harm one never even had to ask to avoid.

To dwell on this name is to notice how much of daily life is carried by a kindness never billed, never repaid, and rarely thanked — and to let that noticing become gratitude rather than mere comfort.

quran_refs.text_ar: إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلُ نَدْعُوهُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْبَرُّ ٱلرَّحِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: Indeed, we used to supplicate[1556] Him before. Indeed, it is He who is the Beneficent, the Merciful."

names.meaning_long:
At-Tawwāb names a reality that turns toward the returning sinner again and again — not once, grudgingly, but repeatedly, without exhausting patience or keeping count against the one who comes back. Al-Ghazālī's treatment emphasizes something easy to miss: the very desire to repent, the prompting that turns a heart back from wrongdoing, is itself understood as a gift preceding the repentance it makes possible. God turns toward the servant before the servant turns toward God.

This reframes repentance from a transaction the servant initiates and God merely ratifies, into something closer to a conversation God opens first. The proper response is not simply to repent once decisively, but to let turning back become an ordinary, repeated posture of life — and to extend the same patient welcome toward others who stumble.

quran_refs.text_ar: فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ كَلِمَـٰتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ
quran_refs.text_en: Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words,[22] and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of Repentance,[23] the Merciful.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muntaqim describes a just requital against persistent, unrepented wrongdoing — not a reactive anger, since nothing provokes or unsettles the one this name describes, but a settled justice that eventually meets oppression and defiance with consequence. It is invoked far less often than the names of mercy, and deliberately so: classical treatment reads it as the exception that proves how overwhelmingly mercy predominates, surfacing only where mercy has been repeatedly, knowingly refused.

Read alongside Al-'Afūw and Ar-Raḥmān, this name is not meant to frighten the sincere but to reassure the wronged: tyranny does not simply vanish into the historical record unanswered.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن ذُكِّرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ ثُمَّ أَعْرَضَ عَنْهَآ ۚ إِنَّا مِنَ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ مُنتَقِمُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: And who is more unjust than one who is reminded of the verses of his Lord; then he turns away from them? Indeed We, from the criminals, will take retribution.

names.meaning_long:
Al-'Afūw is often distinguished from the earlier name Al-Ghafūr: where maghfira (forgiveness) suggests a covering-over, a concealment of the sin, 'afw suggests something stronger — an effacing, as though the wrong had never been recorded at all. Al-Ghazālī's characteristic attention to fine distinctions between near-synonymous names suggests he reads this as forgiveness's fullest form: not merely withheld punishment, but erasure.

For the one who has wronged themselves repeatedly and fears the weight of an accumulating record, this name offers something more complete than a suspended sentence — the possibility that sincere return truly wipes the page clean.

quran_refs.text_ar: فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا
quran_refs.text_en: For those it is expected that Allāh will pardon them, and Allāh is ever Pardoning[214] and Forgiving.

names.meaning_long:
Ar-Ra'ūf sits close to Ar-Raḥīm in meaning but is often read as naming a particularly tender, almost pre-emptive compassion — a gentleness that shields from hardship before it strikes rather than only consoling after. It is the kind of care that anticipates need.

Held together with the earlier mercy-names in this corpus, Ar-Ra'ūf rounds out a vocabulary of compassion that the tradition takes pains not to flatten into a single word: mercy that gives (Ar-Raḥmān), mercy that responds (Ar-Raḥīm), and here, mercy that gently protects.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْرِى نَفْسَهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ
quran_refs.text_en: And of the people is he who sells himself, seeking means to the approval of Allāh. And Allāh is Kind to [His] servants.

names.meaning_long:
Mālik al-Mulk names an ownership of dominion so complete that every throne, every office, every measure of worldly power is held only on loan — granted to whom this reality wills and stripped from whom it wills, answerable to no one. Al-Ghazālī frequently returns to the Qur'anic verse invoking this name directly ("Say: O Allah, Owner of Sovereignty, You give sovereignty to whom You will and You take sovereignty from whom You will") as a corrective to the illusion that human power is self-sustained.

Sitting with this name tends to relativize ambition and fear alike: the rise and fall of rulers, empires, and one's own small measure of standing all trace back to a sovereignty that was never really anyone else's to keep.

quran_refs.text_ar: قُلِ ٱللَّهُمَّ مَـٰلِكَ ٱلْمُلْكِ تُؤْتِى ٱلْمُلْكَ مَن تَشَآءُ وَتَنزِعُ ٱلْمُلْكَ مِمَّن تَشَآءُ وَتُعِزُّ مَن تَشَآءُ وَتُذِلُّ مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ بِيَدِكَ ٱلْخَيْرُ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
quran_refs.text_en: Say, "O Allāh, Owner of Sovereignty, You give sovereignty to whom You will and You take sovereignty away from whom You will. You honor whom You will and You humble whom You will. In Your hand[121] is [all] good. Indeed, You are over all things competent.

names.meaning_long:
This name joins two qualities that might seem to pull in different directions: jalāl, a majesty and greatness that inspires awe and a certain trembling before what so exceeds us, and ikrām, a generosity and honoring that draws the servant near rather than away. Al-Ghazālī's treatment of paired compound names often reads them as deliberately holding together what the heart needs held together — reverence and intimacy, fear and hope, as two aspects of a single sound relationship with God rather than competing moods.

To dwell on Dhū al-Jalāl wal-Ikrām is to resist collapsing faith into either pure awe (which can curdle into dread) or pure familiarity (which can curdle into presumption), and instead to let majesty and generosity correct each other.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَيَبْقَىٰ وَجْهُ رَبِّكَ ذُو ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ
quran_refs.text_en: And there will remain the Face[1597] of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor.

quran_refs.text_ar: تَبَـٰرَكَ ٱسْمُ رَبِّكَ ذِى ٱلْجَلَـٰلِ وَٱلْإِكْرَامِ
quran_refs.text_en: Blessed is the name of your Lord, Owner of Majesty and Honor.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Muqsiṭ is close in territory to the earlier name Al-'Adl but is often read with a particular emphasis on restoring and redressing rights — giving every wronged party their due from whoever wronged them, especially in the ultimate reckoning where no earthly court remains to settle accounts. Where 'adl names justice as a governing quality, qisṭ leans toward justice enacted, rights returned, and balance actively restored rather than merely maintained.

For anyone who has watched a wrong go unanswered in this life, the name carries a quiet promise: nothing of that imbalance is forgotten or lost.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Jāmi' names the one who gathers what has scattered — bodies dispersed across the earth and across time, reassembled whole on the Day of Judgment despite every apparent impossibility of the task. Al-Ghazālī's discussion of this name is understood to extend the sense of "gathering" beyond resurrection alone, to the deeper ordering by which fitting things are brought together throughout creation: causes matched to effects, souls matched to what suits them.

The name offers a particular comfort against the anxiety of loss and separation — nothing genuinely dispersed, whether a scattered body or a scattered life, is beyond being gathered again.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Ghanī names a completeness that requires nothing outside itself — not richness in the sense of abundant possessions, which could always in principle run out, but the absolute absence of need of any kind. Al-Ghazālī is understood to distinguish this unqualified self-sufficiency sharply from any creaturely wealth, however vast, since even the wealthiest person remains dependent on food, health, and time not their own.

Everything else, by contrast, needs this reality entirely — a dependence so total that even a creature's next breath is not self-sustained. The honest response to this asymmetry is not despair but a kind of relief: the aim of a spiritual life is not to achieve self-sufficiency but to rightly locate one's dependence.

quran_refs.text_ar: فِيهِ ءَايَـٰتٌۢ بَيِّنَـٰتٌ مَّقَامُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۖ وَمَن دَخَلَهُۥ كَانَ ءَامِنًا ۗ وَلِلَّهِ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ حِجُّ ٱلْبَيْتِ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا ۚ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ
quran_refs.text_en: In it are clear signs [such as] the standing place of Abraham. And whoever enters it [i.e., the Ḥaram] shall be safe. And [due] to Allāh from the people is a pilgrimage to the House - for whoever is able to find thereto a way. But whoever disbelieves [i.e., refuses] - then indeed, Allāh is free from need of the worlds.[140]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Mughnī names the one who grants sufficiency and lifts want — the source to whom Al-Ghanī's self-sufficiency corresponds when directed outward toward creation. Whatever independence a person attains from need of others ultimately traces back to this enriching, since no one enriches themselves from nothing.

Read together with Al-Ghanī, the pair moves from a statement about God's own nature to a statement about how that nature acts toward creatures — the source of all sufficiency is also the one who supplies it.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Māni' names a withholding that belongs to wisdom and mercy rather than to stinginess — restraining harm before it reaches someone, or withholding a request whose granting would not, in the larger scheme, have served the one asking. Classical discussion of this name is careful to set it apart from any suggestion of a grudging or diminished generosity; what is withheld is withheld precisely because giving it would not have been kindness.

This reframes unanswered prayers and closed doors not necessarily as refusal, but sometimes as a form of protection whose reasoning is not always visible from where the request was made.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Aḍ-Ḍārr and its companion An-Nāfi' together affirm that both hardship and benefit trace, in the end, to a single governing wisdom rather than to independent forces of good and ill fortune. Al-Ghazālī's broader theological commitments hold that nothing in creation causes harm or benefit by its own independent power; illness, loss, and adversity occur only by leave of the reality this name describes, and even apparent harm may carry a hidden benefit — purification, reminder, or elevation — not visible from inside the difficulty itself.

This is a name approached with particular care: it is never invoked to excuse cruelty or to explain away suffering glibly, but to locate all adversity, however painful, within a governance that is never arbitrary.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
An-Nāfi' names the source of all benefit — every advantage gained through medicine, effort, or circumstance ultimately traces to a benefit-granting reality rather than to the independent efficacy of the apparent cause. Paired with Aḍ-Ḍārr, this name completes a single teaching: neither harm nor benefit belongs to secondary causes in their own right.

The practical fruit of this name is a redirection of gratitude — toward the true source of every good outcome, rather than only toward the visible means by which it arrived.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
An-Nūr is one of the richest chapters in al-Ghazālī's engagement with the Names, and he is understood to treat it not as physical light but as that by which things become manifest and knowable at all — light as the very condition of visibility and understanding, rather than an object seen. On this reading, God is "Light" in the sense of being the ultimate source of all illumination: the light that lets the physical eye see, and, more importantly, the light that lets the inner eye of the heart perceive truth, guidance, and reality itself.

This metaphorical, non-physical reading of light recurs across his other writings on the subject, and it reframes guidance and insight — a sudden clarity in the heart, an unlooked-for understanding — as instances of this one Light reaching a particular soul, rather than as achievements of unaided human reasoning.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱللَّهُ نُورُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ مَثَلُ نُورِهِۦ كَمِشْكَوٰةٍ فِيهَا مِصْبَاحٌ ۖ ٱلْمِصْبَاحُ فِى زُجَاجَةٍ ۖ ٱلزُّجَاجَةُ كَأَنَّهَا كَوْكَبٌ دُرِّىٌّ يُوقَدُ مِن شَجَرَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ زَيْتُونَةٍ لَّا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلَا غَرْبِيَّةٍ يَكَادُ زَيْتُهَا يُضِىٓءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نَارٌ ۚ نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ ۗ يَهْدِى ٱللَّهُ لِنُورِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ لِلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ
quran_refs.text_en: Allāh is the Light[996] of the heavens and the earth. The example of His light[997] is like a niche within which is a lamp;[998] the lamp is within glass, the glass as if it were a pearly [white] star lit from [the oil of] a blessed olive tree, neither of the east nor of the west, whose oil would almost glow even if untouched by fire. Light upon light. Allāh guides to His light whom He wills. And Allāh presents examples for the people, and Allāh is Knowing of all things.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Hādī names a guidance that operates on two levels at once: an outward guidance given to every creature toward what suits its nature (the instinct that leads a creature to its sustenance, its habitat, its manner of life) and an inward guidance given specifically to the human heart toward true faith and the straight path. Al-Ghazālī's treatment is understood to place special weight on this second, inward sense — the guidance that reaches past hearing a message to actually accepting and living by it, which no messenger or book can supply on its own.

This distinguishes Al-Hādī from the mere provision of external means of guidance (revelation, reminders, good counsel): the name points to whatever it is that turns hearing into believing, a movement the tradition holds is God's alone to grant.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَتُخْبِتَ لَهُۥ قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهَادِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ
quran_refs.text_en: And so those who were given knowledge may know that it is the truth from your Lord and [therefore] believe in it, and their hearts humbly submit to it. And indeed is Allāh the Guide of those who have believed to a straight path.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Badī' names an origination without precedent and without model — bringing things into being with no prior exemplar to imitate and no pre-existing matter to shape, unlike any human maker, who always works from given materials according to a pattern already known or imagined. Al-Ghazālī's discussion of this name is understood to emphasize precisely this contrast: human "originality" is always derivative in a way that ibdā' (true origination) is not.

The name invites wonder rather than mere assent — every genuinely new thing in creation, considered carefully, points back to a making that owes nothing to any prior template.

quran_refs.text_ar: بَدِيعُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ
quran_refs.text_en: Originator of the heavens and the earth. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is.

names.meaning_long:
Al-Bāqī names a continuance of existence entirely free of the possibility of ending — not mere longevity, however great, but a duration unconditioned by decay, since everything else that endures does so only contingently and could, in principle, cease. Al-Ghazālī's treatment is understood to draw this contrast sharply: the heavens, however ancient they may seem, remain the kind of thing that could not-be; only this reality's continuance is not that kind of thing at all.

Set against the fleeting character of every worldly attachment, the name offers not resignation but a reordering of where lasting value is properly placed.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Wārith names the truth that when all creation perishes, every possession and every sovereignty reverts wholly to the one to whom it always, in reality, belonged. Human "ownership" — of land, of wealth, of even one's own body — is on this reading a temporary loan rather than a permanent grant, returned in full when its term ends.

Al-Ghazālī's discussion of this name is understood to draw a sober, practical conclusion from it: since nothing held now is truly kept, the wise course is to hold worldly things loosely and to invest instead in what is not subject to this final inheritance.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ نُحْىِۦ وَنُمِيتُ وَنَحْنُ ٱلْوَٰرِثُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: And indeed, it is We who give life and cause death, and We are the Inheritor.[674]

names.translation: The Guide to the Right Path
names.meaning_long:
Ar-Rashīd is often distinguished from the earlier name Al-Hādī: where Al-Hādī describes guidance given to others, Ar-Rashīd describes a governance of affairs — including God's own decree and design — that unfolds unerringly toward its rightly-ordered end, requiring no counsel, correction, or trial and error along the way. Every plan reaches precisely where perfect wisdom intends, the first time.

Held together with the surrounding names concerning guidance and governance, Ar-Rashīd closes a small cluster of meaning: not only is the servant guided, but the guidance itself, and all that unfolds from it, is never in need of revision.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Aṣ-Ṣabūr names a patience that delays consequence for the disobedient, granting time and space for return, despite having full capacity to act at once. Al-Ghazālī is understood to distinguish this from the earlier name Al-Ḥalīm (Most Forbearing) by a fine but real difference: forbearance withholds out of generosity even where provocation is minimal, while this name's patience persists steadily even under what would, for a creature, be repeated and mounting provocation — yet without the strain or discomfort that the word "patience" implies for a human being.

As traditionally the final name in the sequence al-Ghazālī comments on, it lands with a certain fittingness: after every name of power, justice, and mercy, the last word is one that makes room, patiently, for the reader's own unfinished turning back.

tafsir_links.label: The ḥadīth of the ninety-nine names

names.meaning_long:
Al-Aḥad affirms a oneness stronger than mere numerical singularity — not first in a series that could in principle have had others, but unique in a sense that admits no partner, no likeness, and no second even in conception. Classical discussion often distinguishes this from the nearby name Al-Wāḥid: where Al-Wāḥid denies composition and division (God is not made of parts), Al-Aḥad denies comparison altogether — there is nothing else of which "oneness" could be predicated in the same sense.

Surah al-Ikhlāṣ, the sole Qur'anic reference for this name here, is widely regarded as the most concentrated statement of God's absolute uniqueness in the whole of scripture, and the name draws its full weight from that context.

quran_refs.text_ar: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ
quran_refs.text_en: Say, "He is Allāh, [who is] One,[2011]

names.meaning_long:
Al-Qarīb names a nearness that has nothing to do with physical distance — since the reality this name describes is not located in space to begin with — but with responsiveness and attentiveness to whoever calls out sincerely. The Qur'anic verse most closely tied to this name (2:186) makes the connection explicit: nearness expressed precisely as answering the one who prays, without intermediary or delay.

Read alongside names like Al-Mujīb (the Responsive) and As-Samī' (the Hearing), Al-Qarīb rounds out a picture in which no distance — geographic, social, or moral — actually separates a sincere supplicant from being heard; the perceived distance in difficult moments is not the reality this name describes.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِى عَنِّى فَإِنِّى قَرِيبٌ ۖ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ ٱلدَّاعِ إِذَا دَعَانِ ۖ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لِى وَلْيُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِى لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْشُدُونَ
quran_refs.text_en: And when My servants ask you, [O Muḥammad], concerning Me - indeed I am near. I respond to the invocation of the supplicant when he calls upon Me. So let them respond to Me [by obedience] and believe in Me that they may be [rightly] guided.

quran_refs.text_ar: وَإِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ ۖ هُوَ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱسْتَعْمَرَكُمْ فِيهَا فَٱسْتَغْفِرُوهُ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى قَرِيبٌ مُّجِيبٌ
quran_refs.text_en: And to Thamūd [We sent] their brother Ṣāliḥ. He said, "O my people, worship Allāh; you have no deity other than Him. He has produced you from the earth and settled you in it, so ask forgiveness of Him and then repent to Him. Indeed, my Lord is near and responsive."

names.meaning_long:
Ar-Rabb is one of the most frequent names for God in the Qur'an, opening its very first sūrah ("praise belongs to Allah, Lord of the worlds"), and it carries a dense cluster of meaning — sustaining, nurturing, owning, and governing all at once, closer in feel to "the One who brings each thing to its intended completion" than to "lord" in the sense of mere rank. Its constant Qur'anic presence makes it, in ordinary devotional life, one of the most load-bearing names of all.

Honesty requires a note here that the corpus itself already flags: Ar-Rabb does not appear among the specific ninety-nine names enumerated in either the Tirmidhī or Ibn Mājah lists carried in this app, and it falls outside the traditional list of ninety-nine names that al-Ghazālī's Al-Maqṣad al-Asnā takes as its structure. This entry is therefore offered as a general reflection on a name of unquestioned Qur'anic weight, not as a report of anything specific al-Ghazālī says about it in that particular work — because, to this drafter's knowledge, he does not treat it there as a discrete chapter.

quran_refs.text_ar: ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ
quran_refs.text_en: [All] praise is [due] to Allāh, Lord[4] of the worlds -

names.translation: The Originator of the Heavens and Earth
names.meaning_long:
Al-Fāṭir names an originating that splits being from non-being — the root faṭara carries a sense of "cleaving open" or bringing forth where there was nothing before, close in territory to the earlier names Al-Khāliq (the Creator) and Al-Bāri' (the Maker) and to Al-Badī' (the Incomparable Originator) treated earlier in this set. The Qur'anic verse attached to it here (35:1) opens a sūrah devoted entirely to the wonder of this originating, praised through the imagery of angels' wings and the ordering of the world.

As with Ar-Rabb, honesty is owed here: Al-Fāṭir does not appear among the specific ninety-nine names in either enumeration carried by this corpus, and falls outside the traditional list al-Ghazālī comments on in Al-Maqṣad al-Asnā. Rather than invent a chapter that, to this drafter's knowledge, does not exist there, this entry draws the connection to the closely related names of origination al-Ghazālī does treat, while flagging plainly that this specific name receives no discrete treatment from him in that work.

quran_refs.text_ar: بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ جَاعِلِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ رُسُلًا أُو۟لِىٓ أَجْنِحَةٍ مَّثْنَىٰ وَثُلَـٰثَ وَرُبَـٰعَ ۚ يَزِيدُ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
quran_refs.text_en: [All] praise is [due] to Allāh, Creator of the heavens and the earth, [who] made the angels messengers having wings, two or three or four. He increases in creation what He wills. Indeed, Allāh is over all things competent.
